metadata.raw_response_text:
The keys are the first thing you notice. They hang from Arthur “Artie” Miller’s belt in a heavy, jangling constellation—a brass-and-steel map of every room, locker, and utility closet in Oak Ridge High. For twenty-eight years, that sound has been the metronome of the building. It is the sound that precedes the morning bell and follows the final departure of the faculty.

Artie is sixty-four, with shoulders that have rounded slightly from decades of buffing linoleum and hauling trash bags that weigh as much as a sophomore. He is a man of few words, but he possesses a quiet, encyclopedic knowledge that would baffle the school’s administration. He knows which radiator in the English wing clanks when the temperature drops, which locker 412 has a sticky latch, and, most remarkably, the name of every student who walks through the front doors.

"Morning, Sarah. How’d that chemistry test go?" he says to a girl rushing toward the science lab. A moment later, he nods to a boy in a varsity jacket. "Keep your head up, Marcus. You’ll get the next one."

He doesn't use a roster. He doesn't have a cheat sheet tucked into his pocket. He simply watches. 

"You see them every day," Artie says, leaning against his industrial floor polisher during a brief lull in the cafeteria. "You see them when they’re freshmen, terrified of the hallways, and you see them four years later, walking out the door with a diploma. If you pay attention, you see the whole story. You see the haircuts change, the confidence grow, the heartbreaks. You’d have to be blind not to learn their names."

To the students, Artie is a fixture, as permanent as the brick walls and the smell of floor wax. He is the silent witness to their adolescence. He has mopped up the spills of three decades, cleared the snow from the bleachers, and stood in the shadows of the gym during countless pep rallies. He has seen the school transition from chalkboards to smartboards, from landlines to smartphones, but the fundamental rhythm of the students remains the same.

There is a specific kind of dignity in Artie’s work. He is the first person to arrive and often the last to leave, ensuring the stage is set for the day’s learning. But his real contribution isn't the cleanliness of the floors; it’s the recognition. In a school of twelve hundred students, it is easy to feel like a number, a face in a crowded corridor. Artie’s ability to greet a student by name—to acknowledge their existence—acts as a subtle, steadying anchor. 

"Sometimes," he admits, "a kid is having a bad day. You can see it in their shoulders. Maybe they failed a quiz or had a fight with a friend. If I say, 'Hey, keep your chin up, Leo,' it’s not much. But it’s something. It’s saying, 'I see you. You’re here.'"

Artie has been offered promotions to district-level maintenance, jobs that would take him out of the building and into an office. He has turned them all down. He prefers the hallways. He prefers the noise of the passing periods and the occasional scuff mark on the floor that he can buff away by morning.

As the final bell rings, a wave of teenagers surges toward the exits. The hallway becomes a chaotic river of backpacks and laughter. Artie stands near the main entrance, his keys resting against his hip. He isn't just watching them leave; he is cataloging the day, checking the locks, and preparing for tomorrow. 

"See you tomorrow, Chloe," he says to a girl walking past. 

She smiles, surprised, and waves back. Artie just nods, his hand already reaching for the light switch, ready to bring the building to rest for the night. He has done this for twenty-eight years, and he knows that tomorrow, they will all be back, and he will be here to greet them by name.
